heading: ULLA CORRISPONDENZA DELLUOMO
card_summary: This chapter of the third Canticle of De Harmonia Mundi treats the correspondence (corrispondenza) between man, the angelic intelligences, the heavens, and sacred realities. Zorzi opens by establishing the natural symmetry between men and angels — shared rationality, incorruptibility, and orientation toward the same Lord — drawing on Augustine, Peter's Epistle, and kabbalistic traditions of patriarchal angel-companions (Raziel for Adam, Metatron for Moses, Gabriel for Joseph, etc.). The bulk of the opening portion debates whether man is superior or inferior to the angels, arguing from Paul (Hebrews 1), Psalm 8's Hebrew text (me-elohim, "less than God" not "less than angels"), kabbalistic gematria of the Hebrew word me'at (119), Augustine's image/seal distinction, and Aristotle's logic to conclude that human nature — perfected in Christ — surpasses angelic nature. A third section introduces the theme of angelic envy of man's greater destiny, tracing the devil's seduction of Adam to jealousy of humanity's predestined sovereignty.
essay:
# ULLA CORRISPONDENZA DELLUOMO — Man's Correspondence with the Angels, Heavens, and Sacred Realities

*Note: This summary covers the opening portion of this long section (pp. 2169–2206), specifically chapters 1–3 as they appear in the first ~300 lines of the chunk, corresponding roughly to pp. 2169–2012 of the Bompiani edition. The remainder of the section (treating celestial and sacred correspondences) is not fully represented.*

---

## 1. The Natural Correspondence Between Man and Angels (Cap. I)

Zorzi opens by announcing that having established man's constitution and the grading of his gifts, he now turns to man's *corrispondenza* — symbolic and ontological correspondence — with other creatures, especially the angels. The correspondence is grounded in a shared rational nature: both men and angels participate in rationality (*razionalità*), are oriented toward the same Lord without intermediary, and are in principle incorruptible and eternal. The difference is that angelic eternity is homogeneous and continuous, while human immortality requires a *trasmutazione* — the resurrection transformation described in 1 Corinthians 15 ("Questo elemento corruttibile rivestirà l'incorruttibilità"). Both drink from the same spiritual rock, citing 1 Corinthians 10:4, which Zorzi glosses through Augustine: "Gli angeli lo consultano in quanto Sorgente della vita."

The relationship is compared by analogy: as men train birds for hunting and speech, so angels guide and instruct men, impelling them toward celestial prey and divine eloquence. This is stated in the Latin stratum of the text:

> *Sic ipsi nobis praesidentes regunt impellunt, et instruunt, ut capiamus praedam coelestium, et ut divina eloquia reportemus plus minusve pro nostra capacitate, et pro diligentia quam exhibemus.*

<cite>p. 2002 (Latin column)</cite>

### Kabbalistic Tradition of Patriarchal Angel-Companions

Zorzi then introduces a list drawn, he says, from "le dottrine antichissime degli ebrei" — the ancient Hebrew traditions. Each patriarch had a familiar angel: Adam with **Razi'el**, Shem son of Noah with **Yofi'el**, Isaac and Jacob with **Peli'el**, Joseph with **Gavri'el**, Moses with **Metatron**, Elijah with **Malti'el**. In the New Testament family, Peter had an angel so intimate that when he arrived at Mary's door after his prison escape, the assembled disciples said "È il suo angelo" (Acts 12). John had an angel to reveal the mysteries of Revelation; Cecilia showed her angel to her husband and brother-in-law; Agnes was protected in the brothel by a sensible angel. Francis of Assisi is cited as the paradigmatic modern example, keeping the fast of the archangel Michael and frequenting places propitious to angelic contact (Santa Maria degli Angeli, Monte della Verna).

---

## 2. Is Man Superior to the Angels, or the Reverse? (Cap. II)

This is the most philosophically dense section of the opening portion. Zorzi systematically addresses the question of relative nobility.

**Arguments for angelic superiority** he surveys: (a) Psalm 8's "made him a little lower than the angels" (Augustine's reading); (b) angels are above time, created in an *aeviternitas*, free of matter; (c) angels are *intellectuales* while man is merely *rationalis*; (d) Ezekiel 28:12 calls the angel "signaculum similitudinis" (seal of likeness), seemingly exceeding man's "image of God."

**Zorzi's rebuttal** proceeds on multiple fronts:

**From Paul and Luke:** Angels are "administratorii spiritus… Missi propter eos, qui haereditatem capiunt salutis" (Hebrews 1:14). He who reclines at table is greater than he who serves (Luke 22:27). The angels carried Lazarus's soul — not to help a weak being, but a soul already "sciolta dal corpo e splendida di luce." A child under a tutor is not inferior to the tutor; the child will inherit the kingdom prepared for humanity, not angels, "a constitutione mundi."

**From the order of creation:** Man was the *last* of God's works because he was the *first* in the divine intention — citing the scholastic axiom "Quod est primum in intentione, est ultimum in executione."

**From Christology:** Since Christ-as-man is more noble than the angels (Paul, Hebrews 1: "Tanto melior angelis effectus, quanto differentius prae illis nomen haereditavit"), and by Aristotelian genus logic ("si l'ottimo in questo genere è più nobile dell'ottimo in quel genere, allora questo genere è più nobile di quello"), human nature itself is nobler. The name "Jesus," forged in the Trinity's council, was perfectly fitted to its bearer — a point amplified by noting that God added a letter to Abram's name when he became Abraham, and Adam named creatures according to their properties even with only a "luce fioca."

**The Hebrew text of Psalm 8:** Zorzi's most striking philological intervention cites the Hebrew of Psalm 8:6 in transliteration:

> *wa-techasserehu me'at me-elohim*
> (*vatechasrehua mehat meelohim* in the Latin column)

<cite>p. 2008 (Latin column) / p. 2009 (Italian column)</cite>

He argues that *Elohim* here means "God," not "angels" — the standard rabbinic reading — so the verse says man was made "a little less than God," not than angels. The subsequent phrase "Gli hai dato potere sulle opere delle tue mani" confirms this, since angels too are "opere delle mani di Dio."

**Kabbalistic gematria of *me'at*:** The "secretiores theologi" go further: the Hebrew word *me'at* (מְעַט) has a gematric value of 119 (מ = 40, ע = 70, ט = 9). Zorzi notes this number contains one hundred, one ten, and all units up to (but excluding) ten — "un grande mistero" whose full exposition is deferred.

**The *sigillum* argument:** Ezekiel's "sigillo di somiglianza" (*attah chotem taknit*, Hebrew given in the text) does not outrank the *imago*. Augustine held that God's image is in man "as in a seal," but in Christ "as between father and son." The logic: "il sigillato è sempre più nobile del sigillo stesso." Whether the angel is called a seal of the image or an image of the seal, man-as-the-thing-sealed remains superior.

**The triple intellect:** Zorzi counters the *intellectualis/rationalis* distinction by citing Aristotle ("Homo est potissime intellectus, et ratio") and noting that the Psalms repeatedly ask for *intellectum*. The intellect is threefold: divine-pure, angelic-separate, and human-mixed. At death, the human intellect does not merely reach the angelic level but transcends it to unite with the divine — a union Scripture never attributes to angels:

> *Homo efficitur filius Dei, transformaturque in eandem imaginem, quae est Deus, et cum ipso unitur, quae de angelis, nusquam leguntur.*

<cite>p. 2008 (Latin column)</cite>

---

## 3. Angelic Envy of Man (Cap. III)

The section transitions to a theological explanation of the Fall. The devil's recognition of man's superior image and destiny gave rise to *invidia* — "la tristezza che deriva da un bene altrui" — which Zorzi defines following the scholastic tradition. Citing Wisdom 2:24 ("a causa di questa invidia la morte è entrata nel mondo"), he argues:

> *angelus mortis seduxit, et subiecit hominem, inferens ei mortem, ne perveniret ad eam sortem a qua ipse expulsus erat.*

<cite>p. 2012 (Latin column)</cite>

The devil did not envy God — God is infinitely beyond competition — but he recognized the *gradus* of man and the "perfectissimumque finem ipsi constitutum," including the sovereignty that would flow from the Incarnation. This is grounded in Hebrews 1:6: when the Father "introduced the firstborn into the world, saying: Let all his angels adore him."

---

## Sources and Connections

Throughout these chapters Zorzi draws on: **Scripture** (Ps. 8, 1 Cor. 10 and 15, Heb. 1, Luke 22, Acts 12, Ezek. 28, Wis. 2); **Augustine** (on angels consulting the divine source; on image vs. seal); **Paul's** angel-ministry theology; **Aristotle** (on man as *intellectus et ratio*; the genus-nobility syllogism); **Hebrew Kabbalah** (angel-companion lists, gematria of *me'at*); and **Francis of Assisi** as a contemporary model of angel-intimacy. The argument that man contains within himself the entire world-machine, the angels, and the Archetype ("l'intero edificio del mondo, compresi gli angeli e l'Archetipo") links back to the *microcosm* doctrine elaborated elsewhere in *De Harmonia Mundi* and is central to Zorzi's overarching thesis that universal harmony is expressed most fully in the human being.
